hero.heading: I find what's holding your system back – before it holds your business back.
hero.subtitle: I work with companies whose systems have outgrown their structure. I find what's quietly shaping things — and make sure the system itself enforces the decisions, rather than relying on people, technology, or discipline.
observations.heading: Does any of this sound familiar?
observations.subtitle: Patterns I see in companies time and again.
observations.1.title: Onboarding
observations.1.reality: New hires who take weeks or months before they can really contribute.
observations.2.title: Collaboration
observations.2.reality: People and teams working around each other because there's no shared way of doing things.
observations.3.title: Quality assurance
observations.3.reality: Conventions and standards that only survive as long as someone is watching.
observations.4.title: System development
observations.4.reality: Systems you keep extending instead of restructuring — and every change costs more than the last.
observations.5.title: Knowledge transfer
observations.5.reality: Knowledge that lives in people's heads — and leaves with them.
observations.6.title: Change management
observations.6.reality: Systems that outgrew their original design — and the dependencies are now bigger than any one head can hold.
principles.1.title: Curiosity
principles.1.description: Looking without assumptions — to see what's actually there.
principles.2.title: Understanding
principles.2.description: Grasping how the moving parts interlock.
principles.3.title: Reflection
principles.3.description: Challenging findings until the full picture holds.
principles.4.title: Foresight
principles.4.description: Seeing what the system will need to handle tomorrow.
principles.5.title: Essence
principles.5.description: Stripping away what doesn't serve the solution.
principles.6.title: Endurance
principles.6.description: Building results that hold up over time.
principle.heading: The Decision Enforcement Principle
principle.subtitle: What actually drives my work
principle.para.1: A structural decision holds up for years when it lives in the system — not in people's heads. It keeps working as people move on, technology changes, and priorities shift.
principle.para.2: My approach: every structural decision gets a concrete enforcement point in the system — CI/CD pipelines, automated checks, tool configurations, and policy as code. This is how IT governance becomes part of the system. The team gets feedback right away, on every change and every commit.
principle.para.3: Documentation records the decision. The system enforces it — so nobody has to stand guard. You need both.
principle.para.4: That's the Decision Enforcement Principle. In practice, it shows up in three ways:
principle.facet.1.name: Root cause over symptom
principle.facet.1.statement: Every finding leads back to a specific spot in the system. That's where we start.
principle.facet.1.rationale: Symptoms tell you a pattern's at work. Root causes tell you where it starts. Fix the cause once and the pattern's gone — instead of coming back every week.
principle.facet.2.name: Structure over discipline
principle.facet.2.statement: Rules hold when they sit in the structure. What the system enforces, stays.
principle.facet.2.rationale: Discipline depends on people. Structure is built into the system. Only structure scales — and it holds when the team changes.
principle.facet.3.name: Foundation over workaround
principle.facet.3.statement: I design every solution to outlast people moving on and technology changing.
principle.facet.3.rationale: A decision with a real foundation still holds up three years later. Workarounds have an expiry date — they're no substitute for real structure.
services.heading: How I support you
services.subtitle: Three paths, depending on where you stand.
services.diagnosis.title: Diagnosis
services.diagnosis.subtitle: Straight to the root cause
services.diagnosis.problem: The symptoms are visible — the root cause isn't. You know something needs to change, and you want the right starting point.
services.diagnosis.forwhom.1: Your system landscape has grown organically — and there's no single source of truth about how it fits together.
services.diagnosis.forwhom.2: You change one thing and three others shift with it — because the dependencies aren't easy to trace.
services.diagnosis.forwhom.3: You have decisions to make — and you want more than gut feeling behind them.
services.diagnosis.timeline.1.title: Kick-off & alignment
services.diagnosis.timeline.2.title: Investigation
services.diagnosis.timeline.2.outcome: Layer by layer to the root cause.
services.diagnosis.timeline.2.item.2: Identify root causes behind the visible symptoms
services.diagnosis.timeline.3.title: Findings & handover
services.diagnosis.timeline.3.item.1: Findings report: root causes, impact, urgency
services.diagnosis.delivers.1: Identified root causes with concrete evidence from your system
services.diagnosis.delivers.4: Compact findings report – one to two pages, ready to use
services.diagnosis.objection.q: What if I already know the root cause?
services.diagnosis.objection.a: Then we go deeper on what's already known — and make sure the full picture holds before you commit to a direction.
services.guidance.title: Guidance
services.guidance.subtitle: From root cause to solution
services.guidance.problem: You know where the root cause is. Now you want it resolved — for good, not patched over.
services.guidance.forwhom.1: You've made the decisions and written them down — but the system doesn't enforce them yet.
services.guidance.forwhom.2: Guidelines only hold as long as someone is watching — and discipline alone won't keep them in place.
services.guidance.forwhom.3: Previous solutions haven't held — they come apart the moment someone leaves or the technology changes.
services.guidance.timeline.1.title: Root cause
services.guidance.timeline.1.outcome: The root cause is clear before we begin.
services.guidance.timeline.1.item.1: Find or deepen the root cause
services.guidance.timeline.2.title: Design
services.guidance.timeline.2.outcome: Solution defined before the first change is made.
services.guidance.timeline.3.title: Implementation
services.guidance.timeline.3.outcome: Your team implements – I make sure the direction holds.
services.guidance.timeline.3.item.1: Your team brings their system expertise
services.guidance.timeline.3.item.2: I guide the implementation and keep it on track
services.guidance.timeline.3.item.3: Standards, conventions, and guidelines are enforced by the system — through CI/CD pipelines, automated checks, tool configurations, and policy as code
services.guidance.timeline.4.title: Completion
services.guidance.timeline.4.outcome: The root cause is resolved. Drift surfaces the moment it happens.
services.guidance.timeline.4.item.2: Solution running — visible and automatically checked
services.guidance.delivers.2: Standards, conventions, and guidelines that the system enforces itself
services.guidance.delivers.3: An implementation that stays aligned from start to finish
services.guidance.delivers.4: A result that outlasts people and technologies
services.guidance.objection.q: We don't have an in-house development team – does that still work?
services.guidance.objection.a: Yes. I also work with external teams or freelancers you bring in for the build.
services.sparring.title: Sparring
services.sparring.subtitle: Alignment that grows with your system
services.sparring.problem: Your responsibility covers more ground than any one person can hold in depth — but the calls still land on your desk. You want someone who gets up to speed, watches the direction, and is there when you need a second pair of eyes.
services.sparring.forwhom.1: Your system keeps growing — and the decisions get harder with it.
services.sparring.forwhom.2: Small decisions today compound into structural consequences tomorrow — and you're making them without a second pair of eyes.
services.sparring.forwhom.3: Every structural decision lands on your desk — and the context it needs lives mostly in your head.
services.sparring.timeline.1.title: Onboarding
services.sparring.timeline.1.outcome: I know your system, your standards, and your goals.
services.sparring.timeline.1.item.1: Get to know the system, context, and goals
services.sparring.timeline.2.title: Feedback & alignment
services.sparring.timeline.2.item.1: Evaluate decisions in the full system context
services.sparring.timeline.3.title: Deep dive
services.sparring.timeline.3.item.1: Evaluate new developments in the context of the system
services.sparring.delivers.2: Ongoing alignment that grows with your system
services.sparring.delivers.3: A dedicated partner who knows your system
services.sparring.delivers.4: Confidence in decisions that reach beyond your core expertise
services.sparring.objection.q: How long until you really know my system?
services.sparring.objection.a: That depends on the complexity. In the first month I get up to speed – after that I know the context that matters. The onboarding is part of the retainer.
about.heading: Philipp Höllinger
calltoaction.heading: Does any of this resonate?
calltoaction.subtitle: Then let's talk.
footer.tagline: Decisions that outlast people, technologies, and discipline — enforced by the system itself.
about.who.heading: Systems Architect · Austria
about.who.1: I spot patterns before they show up. Step me into a system and within minutes I see the things that will matter down the line — before I can even explain why. It was like that on my first project, and on every one since. I don't stop until I've found a fix that holds for good.
about.who.2: At its core, it's an obsession with the quiet things that shape everything — the small details that decide where a system ends up, and the thrill of watching teams pull in the same direction because the structure lets them.
about.who.3: My career started in logistics — coordinating goods, people, processes. The lesson that stuck: when people with different approaches have to work together, the structure around them is what makes the work run.
about.who.4: After studying computer science, I moved into software engineering and then into architecture — automotive, public sector, software consulting. Architecture was the natural next step. I always wanted the bigger picture because it shapes the details, and the details because they decide whether the picture holds. That's when something clicked: the reliability that's second nature in logistics is exactly what software needs most — and exactly what I set out to bring to the systems I work with.
about.who.5: The more tools, processes, and people interlock, the more it matters that the system itself enforces the decisions. That rarely happens. Structure tends to emerge by accident rather than by design — because guidelines that hold up on their own are rarely planned from day one. Rarely is this ownership formally assigned. So committed teams take it on alongside their core work — with genuine care, but without the structure to make it last.
about.who.6: That's why I founded Curfee — to help companies make their decisions stick by design, so they hold up on their own even when nobody's watching.
observations.1.potential: Team members who hit the ground running – because the system shows them how things work.
observations.2.potential: People and teams moving faster – because the system replaces the need to coordinate.
observations.3.potential: Conventions and standards the system enforces itself.
observations.4.potential: Systems where every improvement builds on the last — because existing decisions are visible and traceable.
observations.5.potential: Decisions that hold — because the system enforces them, not because someone remembers.
observations.6.potential: Changes made with confidence — because the system makes dependencies visible and flags breaks before they ship.
faq.heading: Frequently asked questions
faq.subtitle: Your question isn't here? Bring it to the first conversation.
faq.1.question: Who is this for?
faq.1.answer: For decision-makers working with a system landscape that has outgrown its structure – who want clarity on dependencies, connections, and the root causes behind them.
faq.2.question: How does the first conversation work?
faq.2.answer: No pressure. You tell me what's on your mind, I listen. By the end, we both know whether this is a fit — and what the next step looks like.
faq.3.question: What happens after the diagnosis?
faq.3.answer: You decide. The Diagnosis stands on its own – you can take it from there. If you want, I can take it further with Guidance.
faq.4.question: Do you implement yourself?
faq.4.answer: No. I design the solution and guide your team through the build. The actual implementation stays with the people who know the systems best.
faq.5.question: Do you work remotely or on-site?
faq.5.answer: Both — it depends on the situation. Most of the work happens remotely, and I come on-site when it makes sense.
faq.6.question: Do you work within agile frameworks?
faq.6.answer: No. I work alongside your team's rhythm — the analysis itself needs more depth than a single sprint allows.
faq.7.question: What is the Decision Enforcement Principle?
faq.7.answer: The Decision Enforcement Principle is the core of my work: the system itself has to enforce structural decisions — through CI/CD pipelines, automated checks, tool configurations, and policy as code — so that IT governance becomes part of the system, not a matter of discipline or memory. What's wired into the system holds up, even when people or technologies change.
faq.8.question: Who is Philipp Höllinger?
faq.8.answer: Philipp Höllinger is a systems architect based in Austria and the founder of Curfee (2026). His background spans logistics, software engineering, and enterprise architecture across the automotive, public sector, and software consulting industries. He lives in Salzburg and advises CTOs, engineering leaders, and founders in the DACH region.
consent.message: This website uses cookies for analytics.
cookies.heading: Cookie Policy
cookies.what.heading: What are cookies
cookies.what: Cookies are small text files stored on your device when you visit a website. They serve to make websites more user-friendly and efficient. Cookies fall into two categories: first-party cookies, which are set by the website being visited, and third-party cookies, which are set by external services integrated into the website.
cookies.legal.heading: Legal basis
cookies.legal: The use of cookies is governed by § 165(3) of the Austrian Telecommunications Act 2021 (TKG 2021), which implements Art. 5(3) of the ePrivacy Directive. Strictly necessary cookies are set on the basis of the legitimate interests of the data controller pursuant to Art. 6(1)(f) GDPR, as they are required for the proper operation of the website. Non-essential cookies are only set with the explicit consent of the user pursuant to Art. 6(1)(a) GDPR.
cookies.types.heading: Types of cookies used
cookies.types.necessary.label: Necessary cookies
cookies.types.necessary: These cookies are required for the basic functionality and security of the website. They are set without consent. Legal basis: Art. 6(1)(f) GDPR.
cookies.types.analytics.label: Analytics cookies (Google Analytics)
cookies.types.analytics: These cookies are used to measure website usage and to improve the website based on usage data. They are only set with the explicit consent of the user. Legal basis: Art. 6(1)(a) GDPR. Provider: Google Ireland Limited, Gordon House, Barrow Street, Dublin 4, Ireland. Cookie storage period in browser: 2 years. Data retention: 14 months. Further details are provided in the privacy policy.
cookies.list.heading: Cookie list
cookies.list.name: Name
cookies.list.gastar.name: _ga_*
cookies.manage.heading: Managing cookies
cookies.manage: Cookies can be managed and deleted at any time via the browser settings. Consent to non-essential cookies can be withdrawn at any time. Instructions for managing cookies can be found in the help sections of the respective browser.
cookies.updates.heading: Updates to this policy
cookies.updates: This cookie policy may be updated to reflect changes in the cookies used or for legal reasons. The date of the last update is shown at the top of this page.
privacy.cookies.reference: Details on the cookies used on this website are provided in the cookie policy.
privacy.heading: Privacy Policy
privacy.phone.label: Phone
privacy.scope: This privacy policy informs users of this website, in accordance with Art. 13 of the General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR), about the nature, scope, and purpose of the processing of personal data by the data controller.
privacy.controller.heading: Data controller
privacy.personal.heading: Processing of personal data
privacy.personal: Personal data is collected on this website only to the extent that is technically necessary. Processing is subject to purpose limitation and data minimisation. Collected data is under no circumstances disclosed to third parties without a legal basis. The processing of personal data is based on the following legal grounds: Consent pursuant to Art. 6(1)(a) GDPR — the data subject has given consent to the processing of their personal data for one or more specific purposes. Performance of a contract pursuant to Art. 6(1)(b) GDPR — processing is necessary for the performance of a contract to which the data subject is party, or in order to take steps at the request of the data subject prior to entering into a contract. Legitimate interests pursuant to Art. 6(1)(f) GDPR — processing is necessary for the purposes of the legitimate interests pursued by the controller or by a third party, except where such interests are overridden by the interests or fundamental rights and freedoms of the data subject.
privacy.rights.heading: Rights of data subjects
privacy.rights: As a data subject, you have the following rights: Right of access (Art. 15 GDPR) — You have the right to obtain confirmation as to whether personal data is being processed, as well as information about such data and its processing. Right to rectification (Art. 16 GDPR) — You have the right to request the rectification of inaccurate or incomplete personal data. Right to erasure (Art. 17 GDPR) — You have the right to request the erasure of your personal data, subject to the conditions laid down in Art. 17 GDPR. Right to restriction of processing (Art. 18 GDPR) — You have the right to request the restriction of processing of your personal data. Right to data portability (Art. 20 GDPR) — You have the right to receive your personal data in a structured, commonly used, and machine-readable format. Right to object (Art. 21 GDPR) — You have the right to object at any time, on grounds relating to your particular situation, to the processing of personal data concerning you.
privacy.withdrawal.heading: Right to withdraw consent
privacy.withdrawal: Pursuant to Art. 7(3) GDPR, you have the right to withdraw consent at any time. The lawfulness of processing carried out on the basis of consent before its withdrawal is not affected. Withdrawal can be declared by email.
privacy.complaint.heading: Right to lodge a complaint
privacy.complaint: If you believe that the processing of your personal data violates the GDPR, you have the right, pursuant to Art. 77 GDPR, to lodge a complaint with the competent supervisory authority.
privacy.ssl.heading: SSL/TLS encryption
privacy.ssl: For security reasons and to protect the transmission of personal data, this website uses SSL or TLS encryption. An encrypted connection can be identified by the address beginning with 'https://' in the browser's address bar.
privacy.cookies.heading: Cookies
privacy.links.heading: Links to third-party websites
privacy.links: This website contains links to third-party websites. When accessing these external links, data may be transmitted to the respective operator. The controller accepts no liability for the content and data processing by third parties. Users are encouraged to review the privacy policies of linked websites.
privacy.hosting.heading: Hosting
privacy.hosting: This website is hosted on GitHub Pages, a service provided by GitHub, Inc., 88 Colin P Kelly Jr St, San Francisco, CA 94107, USA. When accessing this website, information is automatically recorded in so-called server log files. This includes: IP address, date and time of access, requested resources, browser and operating system used. This data is stored temporarily for security purposes and deleted after a short period. Processing is based on the legitimate interest pursuant to Art. 6(1)(f) GDPR to ensure the uninterrupted operation of the website. For the transfer of personal data to the USA (third country), GitHub employs standard contractual clauses pursuant to Art. 46(2)(c) GDPR. GitHub is also certified under the EU-US Data Privacy Framework.
privacy.analytics.heading: Google Analytics
privacy.analytics: This website uses Google Analytics, a web analytics service provided by Google Ireland Limited, Gordon House, Barrow Street, Dublin 4, Ireland. Google Analytics is activated exclusively with the consent of the user pursuant to Art. 6(1)(a) GDPR. Consent preferences are stored for 30 days. The following data is collected: pages visited and timestamps, interactions on the website, traffic source, device and browser information, geographic information (country/region), IP address (anonymized). Data is retained for 14 months. Google employs standard contractual clauses pursuant to Art. 46(2)(c) GDPR and is certified under the EU-US Data Privacy Framework.
privacy.analytics.more: Further information on how Google Analytics handles user data can be found in Google's privacy policy.
